@denzelw2 if you look at the example on https://dpkt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples.html#examples-in-dpkt-examples you can see the code pulling out the fields in the packet including the src and dst ip address. So you can take that example code as a starting point, add a Counter object and simply add up the packets for each unique ip you see.

```
In [1]: from collections import Counter
In [2]: ip_counter = Counter()
In [3]: ip_counter['192.168.0.1'] += 1
In [4]: ip_counter['192.168.0.2'] += 1
In [5]: ip_counter['192.168.0.1'] += 1
In [6]: ip_counter
Out[6]: Counter({'192.168.0.1': 2, '192.168.0.2': 1})
```

That's about as much help as we can give here.. the project is focused on DPKT not python programming in general. Cheers and best of luck!

The issue still exists.

When the user builds an empty IPv6 packet and output it as string, he may write the statement below.

``` python
packet = dpkt.ip6.IP6()
print str(packet)
```

Then we'll get the following exception.
``` python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "E:\dpkt_laptop\hao_test\issue94.py", line 4, in <module>
    str(packet)
  File "F:\Python27\lib\site-packages\dpkt-1.8.6.2-py2.7.egg\dpkt\ip6.py", line 113, in __str__
    if (self.p == 6 or self.p == 17 or self.p == 58) and not self.data.sum:
AttributeError: 'IP6' object has no attribute 'p'
```

This is because we didn't call `unpack` method if we use the default constructor of `ip6.py`. Therefore `IP6` object won't have the attribute of `p` and `extension_hdrs`. However definitely in `str()` we'll use the two attributes, please check the following code of `str()`.

``` python
def __str__(self):
        if (self.p == 6 or self.p == 17 or self.p == 58) and not self.data.sum:
            # XXX - set TCP, UDP, and ICMPv6 checksums
            p = str(self.data)
            s = dpkt.struct.pack('>16s16sxBH', self.src, self.dst, self.nxt, len(p))
            s = dpkt.in_cksum_add(0, s)
            s = dpkt.in_cksum_add(s, p)
            try:
                self.data.sum = dpkt.in_cksum_done(s)
            except AttributeError:
                pass
        return self.pack_hdr() + self.headers_str() + str(self.data)
```

Up to now, the issue should be clear. Thus the question is, is the usage of `packet = dpkt.ip6.IP6()` valid? If so, we need to update the constructor or `str` to fix this bug.

As for choosing a license, it is up to you as the author, but remember to think about licenses when copying code from other projects. For example, Spotmop includes some code from Mopidy-Mopify which is Apache 2-licensed. If you want to keep that code around, you're limited to using Apache 2 or any compatible license yourself.

We (as in the Mopidy project) tend to recommend permissive licenses (Apache/MIT/X11/BSD) and also require that for a project to be listed in our docs. That test is the base class for all the backends, so every backend tests getting and setting a password where the 'system', 'username', and 'password' are all unicode characters (including russian, hebrew, and others).

Perhaps the umlaut fails because it's being passed in another encoding (latin-1, for example).

We should try putting together a test case that reproduces the issue. It's possible it's been solved.

Yes, sure, but the new `Mesh` type requires some more love. Are you sure `field_data` still makes sense there as a generic attribute? Why you added `node_sets` as a generic attribute (only used in exodus) ? And finally, there is no general mechanism  or "protocol" yet so we can pass around extra stuff like Gmsh periodicity. All these details have to be decided eventually. 

So, I'll start with  a quick proposal to help updating this PR:

We can define `Mesh` this way:

```python
class Mesh(object):
    def __init__(self, ..., **kwargs):
        ...
        self.__dict__.update(kwargs)
```

and then the Gmsh reader can do:

```
def read(...):
    return Mesh(..., gmsh_periodic=periodic)
```

Are you OK with that approach?

Hi guys,

I am experiencing this same issue and believe I found the problem.  Pip installed flask_restful_swagger-2==0.35 which does not contain a _extract_schemas method within flask_restful_swagger_2.py:Api(restful_Api).  If I force pip to install flask_restful_swagger-2==0.33 and inspect that object I see that it does contain the appropriate method.

Regarding the new FRS version, it exists in _BaseExtractorImpl(Extractor) which is in the same module but definitely not in the class.  I could not see where any extractor was wired in to the current object.  I suppose the questions are 1) whether the FRS guys provided a migration path for this method and 2) whether your deployment script is being properly executed through tools such as pip, including evaluation of dependencies.

For simplicity and familiarity Stream based interfaces are good. However for larger datasets perhaps a compromise can be reached between simplicity and performance by standing on the shoulders of something like [BerkeleyDB](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/bsddb3/6.1.1) which will address two issues:

 - Parallel writes/modifications to the data
 - Having to rewrite the entire dataset when one single element is changed
 - Transactions! :heart_eyes: 

A plus point is that it is already battle tested in terms of performance. To the best of my knowledge BerkeleyDB is relatively efficient in terms of modifications, and being a key-value database it already fits TinyDB quite well.

This will really make TinyDB more suitable for bigger datasets and combined with the nice API that it already provides will pack quite a punch.

When guess_matches calls sanitize on title, we get the same 'expected string or buffer', this time from the re module.

The fix is easy (I found the if/elif pattern is better for readability):
def guess_matches(video, guess, partial=False):
...
        if video.series and 'title' in guess:
            series_guess = guess['title']
            if isinstance(series_guess, six.string_types) and sanitize(series_guess) == sanitize(video.series):
                matches.add('series')
            elif isinstance(series_guess, list) and any(sanitize(t) == sanitize(video.series) for t in series_guess):
                matches.add('series')
...

The additional flexibility helps for systems that do not include TZ information. For example, the Chicago Transit Authority API sends back a string in the following format: '20171203 08:53'. This is in 'US/Central', but maya passes it into pendulum which assumes 'UTC' by default.

I'm calculating the arrival time of the next bus, so adding a bit more context does make my life a bit easier:

```console
>>> import maya
>>> now = maya.now()
>>> format(now)
'2017-12-03 14:41:20.464678+00:00'
>>>
>>>
>>> without_tz = maya.parse('20171203 08:53')
>>> format(without_tz)
'2017-12-03 08:53:00+00:00'
>>> (without_tz.epoch - now.epoch) / 60.0
-348.3333333333333
>>>
>>>
>>> with_tz = maya.parse('20171203 08:53', timezone='US/Central')
>>> format(with_tz)
'2017-12-03 14:53:00+00:00'
>>> (with_tz.epoch - now.epoch) / 60.0
11.666666666666666
```

If this is not a valid use case of maya, I can close the PR and use pendulum directly.

I had tried to pip install from git and update the schedule, as others have mentioned, but I was still having issues keeping the games up to date. Here is what I did to get it working reliably on a Heroku environment using pipenv.
-------------------------------

There is a line in `nflgame/live.py` that is using the post season url and not the regular season url to update `schedule.json` 🙀.

So in `nflgame/live.py`, line 73 needs to be commented out and line 68 commented in. 

I believe if you do that, then it doesn't matter that you're using an older version of `schedule.json` since it will be updated dynamically from the URL (so using the latest git version doesn't matter). If the file isn't updating because it says it's already up to date, you can edit the timestamp at the very bottom of `schedule.json` to some time before the game it is you want to update. (For me it was MIA vs TB on Nov 19, 2017 that was missing, 2017111911).

I ended up forking the repo and pipenv installing that version in edit mode instead. 
`pipenv install -e git+https://github.com/denimandsteel/nflgame.git#egg=nflgame`

After that is installed, then you can run:
`python path/to/nflgame/update_sched.py --year 2017` or `nfldb-update --update-schedules` (if you're using nfldb)

This error typically arises when your too low on memory for Binsanity to proceed. Unfortuantely due to the nature of the algorithm there are some high memory constraints. Typically our lab caps input at 100,000 contigs when using `Binsanity`. 

I recommend you try running `Binsanity-lc. What this does is it subsamples your contigs using coverage using a less memory intensive method and then implements Affinity Propagation on those subsets. If you want to mimic the command you are running now use this command:

`Binsanity-lc -f ./synth -l samples_splits_f.fasta -c profiles.in` 

Depending on how many threads you can open up at once I'd also recommend using the `--threads` flag with `Binsanity-lc` for the appropriate number available as it will speed up the K-means subsampling step and refinement. 

Let me know if you have any questions!

-- Elaina

For example, it would be useful to know if all the images were grayscale, black and white, or colour (RGB, etc.) - or a mixture of these. This is important because at the very least you want to have images of the same format for input into a neural network (therefore you might want to make all images grayscale). Also, colour RGB images contain three layers, one layer for each colour, and therefore have a matrix of shape `[800x600x3]` for an `800x600` image. Grayscale and Black&White images do not have three layers and therefore have a matrix shape of `[800x600x1]`.

The SQLSTATEs used to be a standard from the Call Level Interface which is overseen by a group of companies, not just Microsoft.  I believe that is still the case.

For others who are curious about how this works:

SQLSTATEs are not arbitrary and the ODBC specification is very specific about them and drivers should all return the same values.  (See, for example, the Diagnostics section of [SQLDriverConnect](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms715433(v=vs.85).aspx).)  They are the mechanism that ODBC uses for reporting errors and the only way we have of differentiating the error types to generate the proper exception type.  (SQLSTATEs are also not Microsoft defined values - they are or were from the Call Level Interface specification level 1 before ODBC existed.  I’m sure MS has added values, but I think they are still a generic standard.)

The driver is actually involved even before the connection has occurred.  It is responsible for establishing the connection as different drivers may use different technologies.  In fact, drivers use different technologies based on the connection string.  For example MySQL may use TCP/IP for remote servers and Unix sockets for local.  SQL Server will use shared memory if the server is local and supports it.  Otherwise it may used named pipes or direct TCP/IP.  Any SQLSTATEs for a failure to connect should be a small set.  Again, see the SQLDriverConnect documentation above.  You will have to do some testing with your DB of choice, but basically any exception when you call pyodbc.connect means the same high-level thing.

There are cases where the driver fails, such as when it cannot be loaded (isn’t installed, etc.), but the SQLSTATEs reported in these cases are from the driver manager, so again they are standardized.

The one exception is when the ODBC error reporting function, SQLGetDiagRec, fails in which case SQLSTATE HY000 is returned.

This is the code that I created. It took me a while because I had no idea about how `--globals-dir` was used, which it is not well specified in the documentation of minizinc about the command `mzn-g12mip`. 

```python
class MySolver(Solver):
    def __init__(self, path=None):
        super().__init__(True, True, globals_dir='linear')
        self.cmd = path or 'flatzinc'

    def solve(self, fzn_file, **kwargs):
        args = [self.cmd, "-b","mip",fzn_file]
        process = run(args)
        return process.stdout
```

> However, I read that you don't take into account volume when filling orders. This is a problem for me since I trade in some fairly illiquid markets. Am I mistaken? Is there actually a way to specify how much of my order will be filled as a function of the volume?

Yes. *Volume* is not considered for order filling. This is a design decision. Let's consider for example a `Fill or Kill` order. To avoid writing something up, let me quote *Interactive Brokers*

  - https://www.interactivebrokers.com/en/index.php?f=586

> Setting FOK as the time in force dictates that the entire order must execute immediately or be canceled. A trader might see a short-lived opportunity to buy or sell a stock or option that would suit a strategy or fit within a portfolio. However, the time opportunity might be subject to buying or selling a minimum number of shares or options. The fill-or-kill order type is designed to ensure that the investor does not receive a partial fill that would not suit his current appetite. Failure to fill the entire order upon immediate submission to the market causes the system to cancel the order in its entirety.

*backtrader* tries to offer a realistic approach. The statement above can only be filled if you are operating with **tick data**, because that would be the only possible way to ensure that a `Fill or Kill` order is either *filled* or immediately *killed* if a *partial match* would be received.

Corollary: *Real volume matching does only make sense when working with tick data*

Taking that into account, the following was added to the decision:

  - *Do not trade markets which are not liquid enough to immediately fully absorb an order*

With all that in mind, the % based approach to limit volume usage of a bar for order matching wouldn't hurt and as such I think it can get a try.

> If not, I am willing to write a class that adds that functionality.

This wouldn't be a class but part of the existing matching logic of the *broker*. The *broker* can (and does with live feeds) report partial executions and this would simply be the case: *a partial execution*

Note: This works with Windows Console.  Powershell appears to mess with the encoding when using pipelines (as described a few posts earlier).

-----

Here is the situation as I understand it (documented here for future reference).

From the [pandoc documentation](http://pandoc.org/README.html):

> Pandoc uses the UTF-8 character encoding for both input and output.

Pandoc-fignos must therefore do the same.  In both python 2 and 3, a call to `sys.stdin.read()` returns a string.  The catch is that strings are different kinds of objects between the two python versions.

[Python 2 strings](https://docs.python.org/2.7/reference/lexical_analysis.html#strings) are represented by a series of bytes (ASCII characters).  Any decoding is left to the programmer.  Since pandoc-fignos mostly passes bytes through to `sys.stdout` there is no ill effect on any platform.

[Python 3 strings](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#strings), on the other hand, are unicode, and so decoding is performed automatically.  The [default expectations](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/sys.html) for encoding depend on the OS:

> The character encoding is platform-dependent. Under Windows, if the stream is interactive (that is, if its isatty() method returns True), the console codepage is used, otherwise the ANSI code page. Under other platforms, the locale encoding is used (see locale.getpreferredencoding()).

UTF-8 is the default encoding of most locales for [many linux distributions](https://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/~mgk25/unicode.html#linux) and [Mac OS X](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mac_OS_Roman#Application_notes).  This is why pandoc-fignos worked with those OSes.  On Windows with python 3 the default encoding is something else, hence we need to enforce pandoc's requirement.  Stackoverflow shows [how to do this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/16549332/python-3-how-to-specify-stdin-encoding).

One final note: The change between python 2 and 3 addressed a variety of problems (viz., ["the change is for the better"](https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.0.html)).  What we have here appears to be an edge case where things were made a little more difficult in python 3.

For tab-completion, here's one idea: just after entering a code (on the `wormhole receive` side), if the user did *not* use tab-completion, print a reminder that it's available:

```
% wormhole rx
Enter receive wormhole code: 6-hazardous-beeswax
(note: you can use <Tab> to complete words)
hello world
%
```

If they *did* use tab, the reminder is not displayed, so we're not cluttering up the output for experienced users.

That example shows one potential downside: when used for text messages (e.g. `wormhole send --text "hello world"`), it might not be obvious which part of the output is the text message, and which part is the tab reminder.

We could also put it into the prompt, but then it will be there every time, even when the user has demonstrated knowledge of the feature:

```
% wormhole receive
Enter receive wormhole code (use <Tab> for completion): 5-liberty-crackdown
hello world
```

@dkg suggested changing the prompt in response to what happens while the user is typing. I don't see how to use this ahead of time (e.g. show the reminder in the prompt at the start, but then remove it from the prompt after they've typed a tab). But maybe we could wait until the user hits Return, and then change the prompt at the very last minute. That would move the reminder up to the code-input line, but wouldn't display anything until we know the reminder might be necessary. I don't know how to make libreadline do that, though: the API I've seen makes the prompt seem pretty immutable.

What I have been using so far to make sure I connect to services like *Redis* is `asyncio.wait_for`.

Here is an example of how I make sure I'm connected:

```python
async def connect():
  connection_made = False

  while not connection_made:
    try:
      connection = await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio_redis.Pool.create(
          host='localhost', port=6379, poolsize=20, db=0), 3.0)
      connection_made = True
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
      print('having trouble connecting to redis')
      # let's try again
      continue

  print('connected to redis')
```

We normally expect queries to be executed via:
`db.get_query_result(...)`

There are some [documented examples](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/database.html#cloudant.database.CloudantDatabase.get_query_result) of iterating docs returned from it.

Note that the default `page_size` is 100, and that `skip` and `limit` are set internally based on this to page results in a memory efficient way (at the expense of making more requests). If you are unconcerned about the memory usage of having more docs at once (especially because you are only using the `_id` field you can increase the `page_size`, reducing the number of requests needed. I suspect that your use of the (internal) `Query` class above used the `limit=100` and explicitly setting the limit higher reduced the number of requests needed to fetch all the query results. It is better to use the `db.get_query_result` as that's really the API for this, for your case something like this should work:

```python
docs = db.get_query_result(selector, fields=["_id"], page_size=5000, use_index="78efbd1fbc0663b7953309184e9c6b3b0c1ca965")
for doc in docs:
    # count
```

maybe too late but for the record. I've fixed this issue with a custom extender in my theme product:

`extender.py`:

```python
from Products.ATContentTypes.interface import IATContentType
from archetypes.schemaextender.interfaces import IBrowserLayerAwareExtender
from archetypes.schemaextender.interfaces import ISchemaModifier
from plone.app.widgets import at
from plone.app.widgets.interfaces import IWidgetsLayer
from zope.component import adapts
from zope.i18nmessageid import MessageFactory
from zope.interface import implements

_plone = MessageFactory('plone')


class MetadataExtender(object):
    """
    """

    implements(ISchemaModifier, IBrowserLayerAwareExtender)
    adapts(IATContentType)
    layer = IWidgetsLayer

    def __init__(self, context):
        self.context = context

    def fiddle(self, schema):
        for field in schema.fields():
            old = field.widget

            if field.__name__ in [
                    'formPrologue', 'formEpilogue',
                    'thanksPrologue', 'thanksEpilogue',
                    'noSubmitMessage']:
                field.widget = at.TinyMCEWidget(
                    label=old.label,
                    description=old.description,
                )

```

`configure.zcml`:

```xml
...
<adapter
      name="plonetheme.yourtheme"
      factory=".extender.MetadataExtender"
      for="Products.ATContentTypes.interface.IATContentType"
      provides="archetypes.schemaextender.interfaces.ISchemaModifier"
      />
...
```

You are correct that the file isn't valid on old, legacy versions of Python. That shouldn't be a problem, because it's perfectly sensible for a package to contain some sources that are only valid on certain versions of Python. The bytecompiling tool should either ignore (and warn about) these failures like Python's own bytecompiling tools do, or allow passing a blacklist of files to skip compiling that you know ahead of time shouldn't be compiled. If it doesn't, you will have to monkeypatch the package to remove the files and replace them with placeholders prior to running the tool.

> would it be possible to support regex when matching things like resources/tags/events etc?

Almost certainly no. The way it works at the moment is that incoming alerts are checked against the blackouts table for any matching "rules". In reality, these rules are just equality matches which means it's fast. Adding support for regex based rules would fundamentally change how this worked. That is, it would be necessary to iterate through every possible rule and evaluate it against the incoming alert which I believe would be prohibitively slow.

> any reason why one cannot create a blackout period for a given environment unless there's at least one outstanding alert currently open for that environment?

There's no reason why a user shouldn't be able to input any environment they want. PR's welcome.

> it would be super useful if one could see the alerts popping up on the dashboard even during a blackout period rather than rejecting them straight-away. I think Alerta should still receive alerts but disable any notification for them?

Blackout handling could be factored out into a plugin then it would be easy to completely disable or reconfigure completely based on your own use case. I'll look at adding this to the next release.

As already said though email, I don't think this should be done in hepcrawl (contents of the email follow).

There are several cases that can arise:
1. The publisher makes available a full structured reference
2. The publisher makes available a list of unstructured references
3. The publisher does not make any reference available in the metadata

In case 1., we don't need need refextract as Hepcrawl can do the
conversion from the publisher's reference format to ours, whereas in
case 3. there is nothing Hepcrawl can do besides providing the PDF.

So case 2. remains, but I think it would be better to have Hepcrawl
populate the raw references in the record, and run refextract (or in
the future maybe Grobid) in the workflow as is done curently to extract
references from PDF. We should have a task there that does reference
extraction from raw references in case they have been provided but
there are no parsed references. In this way, we cleanly separate the
task of translating between metadata formats (Hepcrawl) and parsing
references (refextract) and in the future we can easily swap refextract
for Grobid when it is mature enough.

My understanding of the GPL is that once you pull in any GPL code, the entire codebase gets licensed as GPL. Simply removing the dependency from the codebase does not remove the requirement of licensing the code as GPL - that's the viral bit of the license.

What you've done is just the above. You forked the fuzzywuzzy codebase and changed the import of SequenceMatcher to pull from an external library. Again, I am not a lawyer, but I don't believe that allows you to relicense fuzzywuzzy as MIT.

If that is indeed the case - and if it is, I'd love to see references so I can respond to the occasional email I get from folks about this exact project - then why not submit that single change to this repository?

In short, I don't see any benefits from the indirection here, and only see a further GPL violation.

Please feel free to correct me if I'm wrong - with sources please! - as I personally dislike the GPL and it is very frustrating to me that we have to license it this way in the first place.

Hi @arvind-kalyan, and thanks for checking out pykafka. I'd recommend keeping one producer per topic around for the lifetime of your program. Calling `get_producer` is a bit expensive both in terms of cpu and memory, since it allocates a bunch of new resources that the newly created producer requires. Something like this is probably closer to what you want:
```python
topic_producers = {topic.name: topic.get_producer() for topic in topics_to_produce_to}
for destination_topic, message in consumed_messages:
    topic_producers[destination_topic.name].produce(message)
```
If you have additional questions, please feel free to ask on the [user group](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/pykafka-user) or on this issue. Thanks!

No, I agree, I don't think there's a generalised case like that. What I'm surmising is that the fetcher threads don't get in each other's way at all, it's just that two consumers produce more requests than one. And the end result of that is, that the request queue never quite manages to get to `join`.

I'm quite certain that that `join` is where the main thread blocks: it's waiting for the fetch request queue to get to zero. [Here's](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/files/1695/diff.txt) my print debugging diff: if you run that with one consumer running, and try to exit the interpreter, you can see we enter `RequestHandler.stop` and then exit from it too. You'll also see that the consumer fetcher thread is still live while that happens.

When you run it with two live consumers, the enqueue rate is higher, and when you exit the interpreter you'll see it enter `RequestHandler.stop` but it never exits from there - the `Queue.join` never returns. So I'm not totally sure why that is, but my theory is it's just because the two fetcher threads keep hitting the queue with new requests fast enough.

So under that theory, the reason why the fix in #237 works is only that it makes the two consumers wait a lot, that is,  the locking slows the request rate down. That would be the same as why the longer `sleep` worked. I don't really like it as a solution, because it sacrifices performance when you have multiple consumers, for a problem that only exists at program exit.

[This](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/files/1696/diff.txt) is hackish fix based on that theory. It's not good, it makes for an ugly exit, but it shows that something similar might work.

#!/usr/bin/python

from openshift import client, config

kclient_config = config.new_client_from_config()

Steps to reproduce:

```
$ pip install ipython transaction
$ ipython
In [1]: import transaction

In [2]: from transaction.tests.examples import ResourceManager

In [3]: rm = ResourceManager()

In [4]: def crash(*a, **kw):
   ...:     raise Exception('Boo!')
   ...: 

In [5]: rm.tpc_vote = crash

In [6]: with transaction.manager as txn:
   ...:     txn.join(rm)
   ...:     
```

At the point of crash we're trying to write `u'    txn.join(rm)'` into a `<_io.BytesIO object at 0x7fb0da944110>`.  If we were using cStringIO.StringIO() on Python 2.7, we wouldn't crash (as long as the unicode source line has only ASCII characters in it).

(I'm pretty sure this bug doesn't show up on Python 3 where source lines are always unicode and we use an io.StringIO()).

> The paper I started to look at says the photon shooting is sampling from surface brightness. But, is that after you do a analytic convulsion with one of the built in galaxy shapes?

Every component that is convolved together gets sampled separately.  You start with a photon sampled from the galaxy (say).  Then the photon shooting routine does the convolution by applying the right offset sampled from the PSF.  If the PSF is modeled as multiple components convolved together (e.g. Kolmogorov + Airy) then they each add their own kick.

Verifying that we get equivalent results with both algorithms (fft and phot, and even real-space convolution in some cases) was one of the major ways that we validated the GalSim code.  Shears and other transformations are also implemented differently in the two cases, so agreement there was also part of the validation.

This only breaks down if you need to go to fourier calculations to figure out the surface brightness profile of one of the components.  For a simple Airy, you don't have too -- it's all analytic, even with an obscuration.  But for an aberrated optical PSF, you need an fft.  We currently implement OpticalPSF as an InterpolatedImage under the hood, so it can be photon shot.  But computing the right profile requires an FFT to make that interpolated image object.

I'm trying out multiple versions of swagger-ui, and they seem to behave differently depending on the version.
For reference, here's a curl command that makes sense to me (form parameters passed separately).
```
curl \
  -X POST \
  --header 'Content-Type: multipart/form-data' \
  --header 'Accept: application/json' \
  -F array_values=a \
  -F array_values=b \
  -F array_values=c \
  'http://localhost:8091/test'
```
It looks like the vendored swagger UI creates this equivalent curl command:
```
curl \
  -X POST \
  --header 'Content-Type: multipart/form-data' \
  --header 'Accept: application/json' \
  -F array_values=a%26array_values%3Db%26array_values%3Dc
  'http://localhost:8091/test'
```
Finally, using swagger-ui 3.17.1 the equivalent curl command is:
```
curl \
  -X POST \
  --header 'Content-Type: multipart/form-data' \
  --header 'Accept: application/json' \
  -F "array_values="a","b","c""
  'http://localhost:8091/test'
```
which _almost_ works, but everything is double quoted:
```
['"a"', '"b"', '"c"']
```

A couple things here some of which are critical and some of which are not:

As a more minor point `requirements.txt` does not need have all dependencies frozen into it. `chalice deploy` will explore all the dependencies and attempt to package them up so your `requirements.txt` can just be:
```
pyrebase==3.0.27
```
And it will bundle up all the packages required on a deploy, this would mean you may get different versions of transient dependencies, which is fine if you don't care about their versions. You can of course freeze all the dependencies as you have done if you would like to pin all the transitive dependencies to get a known set of dependencies that won't change out from under you. Just wanted to make sure you were aware of that behavior.


The main issue here is explained by the error message
```
Could not install dependencies:
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.2
pycryptodome==3.4.3
You will have to build these yourself and vendor them in
the chalice vendor folder.
```

The packages `googleapis-common-protos` and `pycryptodome` have C extensions and do not have wheel files on PyPi that allow for automatic packaging on Chalice's part. So you have to build them yourself.
Another issue is that `googleapis-common-protos` looks tricky to install. It depends on you having protoc installed according to the installation instructions: [here](https://github.com/google/protobuf/blob/master/python/README.md#installation) and it does not have wheels available on PyPi so you do need to build and package it yourself on the architecture you intend to run it on, in this case Amazon Linux since that is what Lambda runs on. There are is a tutorial on how to do that  instructions on how to do that located [here](
http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/packaging.html#cryptography-example). You would have to take the extra steps of making sure that the machine you do the build on has `protoc` installed and is able to build that package though by following the instructions in their readme.

You do not need to put everything in the vendor directory, and in fact putting wheel files in the vendor directory is not going to work properly. You can read the packaging documentation that I linked below which explains how to vendor a package.

So in summary, build those two packages on an EC2 instance running Amazon Linux and vendor them according to the documentation I linked below. Let me know if you have any more questions.

Related documentation:
http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/packaging.html
http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/packaging.html#cryptography-example

The more tricky question is can the NEXRAD Level 3 reader perform partial reading of unsupported products.  The Level 3 or NIDS format is a message based file format which can contain radial, Cartesian, and some other more obscure layouts.   Only radial data can be mapped to the **Radar** data model, other formats cannot.  Hence the **NEXRADLevel3** class has only implemented support for reading radial Level 3 data products and raises the NotImplementedError for non-radial data products.  I'm not sure how these could be skipped over exactly, there is no data to map into the **Radar** class so what would the expected return be, an empty **Radar** object?  I think raising the NotImplementedError is more agreeable, you can always catch this error if you are iterating over files.

The FeatureFile 'featureName' tag is specific to ssXX features.  The cvXX FeatureParams field points to a data block with quite a different structure, and different fields; this is why 'ttx' crashes - it is expecting the amount of data associated with the cvXX feature, and is being handed the data block for the ssXX feature instead. makeotf does not yet support building the set of fields associated with the FeatureParams for the cvXX feature.

BTW, extra_fields are as follows. You can add your custom LogRecord members using logging.Filter. E.g., I use the following code:
```python
import logging
import socket


class HostnameFilter(logging.Filter):
    hostname = socket.gethostname()

    def filter(self, record):
        record.hostname = self.hostname
        return True

handler = CloudLoggingHandler(gcloud.logging.Client(), extra_fields=('hostname',))
handler.addFilter(HostnameFilter())
```
Filters are not required to add new fields, they can delete, change, etc.

Sir, why do you like to type many words? Why not just use python and python-docx to make `demo.docx` on the fly.

For future reference, this is what I used to insert bookmarks.

```
from docx import Document
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.text.run import Run

# Configure
style = 'Figure'              # Can be 'Figure', 'Table' or 'Equation'
num = 1

# Initialize
d = Document()
p = d.add_paragraph()

# Insert caption
elem = p._element
r = OxmlElement('w:r')
elem.append(r)
Run(r, p).text = style + ' '

# Insert caption number   
pCaption = OxmlElement('w:fldSimple')
pCaption.set(qn('w:instr'), r' SEQ {style} \* ARABIC '.format(style=style))
elem.append(pCaption)
    
r = OxmlElement('w:r')
pCaption.append(r)
    
rPr = OxmlElement('w:rPr')
r.append(rPr)
    
rPr.append(OxmlElement('w:noProof'))
    
Run(r, p).text = str(num)
```

There are really two things that need doing to have snowball as a good quality shared library.

The obvious one is to make the build system build a shared library.

The other (which hasn't been addressed here) is that the C code snowball currently generates contains a lot of relocations due to the many arrays of string literals.  These result in a load time penalty (as the dynamic loader has to perform these relocations), and a memory overhead (as the pages containing these relocations can't be shared between different processes which have the snowball library loaded).  And the problem gets worse for each new stemmer that is contributed.

For more details of these relocations, see section 2.4.3 of this document:

https://software.intel.com/sites/default/files/m/a/1/e/dsohowto.pdf

I actually solved this in a somewhat primitive but effective way many years ago for the fork of snowball we use in Xapian (the reason why we ended up with a fork was that it was sometimes hard to get Martin to accept patches, especially as he was often very busy with other things).

Since I took over as maintainer I've been gradually merging changes from the fork back in to the main tree, with the hope that eventually Xapian could just use upstream snowball, but this one is still to do.

Here's how I understand how submodules work:

 - all submodules are listed in the `.gitmodules` record file
 - When you `git submodule update --init`, the modules included in the record are cloned using the listed remote
 - `ssh://` remotes in `.gitmodules` rely on your SSH agent for authentication against remote repositories, which is how #3305 came to be.

The issue here isn't with wal-e at all - the issue is with the database's /bin/boot logic, which will only perform a restore/replay if it detects that it has no data. If the database comes up on a host which previously had the database running, it happily starts serving "old" data, even though newer backup data exists.

@msarahan  TL;DR add  `version=”1.1”` to your load routine:  `round_trip_load(version="1.1")` (or `load(stream, Loader=ruamel.yaml.RoundTripLoader, version="1.1")`

The "official ref card" is the refcard for PyYAML which only supports YAML1.1 completely and that and some other features were dropped in the [YAML 1.2 specification](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html) (back in 2009), which IMO you should follow. I recently made ruamel.yaml (already with better support for 1.2 than PyYAML) version aware [as described here](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2077#issuecomment-189766037) and announced  the top of the README that it defaults to 1.2. 

After loading (with version="1.1") this will be booleans internally undistinguishable from ones loaded from true or false, and on dumping these will all be dumped as true resp. false. It is probably possible to dump booleans as Yes/No with a little code grafting, but I am not a fan of that, especially as there are also people that are confused that you need to quote Yes, etc if you specify a scalar and want it to load as a string (using `load` or `safe_load`), which is an extra reasons to default to 1.2.
 
With `version=1.1` the YAML code in your tests seem to load fine:

> **index.routing.allocation.exclude.{attribute}**

If you use an arbitrary value, none of the nodes should have that value, so the shard can be assigned there.

Strange.  It seems you do not have DEBUG mode on the master node, so we're left with only INFO.

The bit that interests me is that you do not see the delete request, only the node re-initializing.

Also that you're on 1.7.0.  There were a few bugs with 1.7.0 which may or may not have anything to do with what you're seeing.  It can't hurt to upgrade to 1.7.2, though.

> Elasticsearch version is: 1.1.1

That may be some of the issue.  Elasticsearch is on version 2.2.0 right now, with 1.1.1 being a year or so old.  Snapshots were only added in 1.0.0, and many bugs and issues have been squashed since then.  

Versions less than 1.4 are deprecated and no longer supported, so I highly recommend upgrading—carefully and methodically—to more recent versions in stages.  The reason for this is that your 1.1.1 indices may not be recognized in a direct upgrade to 2.2.0.  You may have to go from 1.1.1 to 1.4.4, to 1.7.3, to 2.2.0, or something like that.  You may also have to run an optimize (don't forceMerge to 1 on the first time, unless they're already optimized) in between to get _new_ segments created which will be recognized in newer versions of Elasticsearch.

I'm not 100% sure of your upgrade path, but you can certainly find out by asking at https://discuss.elastic.co/c/elasticsearch

def create_vm_deployment(subscription_id,      # subscription ID
        certificate_path,           # path to Azure management certificate (pem)
        cloud_service_name, 
        ssh_certificate_thumbprint, # SHA1 thumbprint of SSH certificate in the cloud service
        storage_account,
        storage_container,
        os_image_name,              # OS image name, retrieved by /services/images
        user_name,
        password,
        host_name,
        vm_size):
    
    sms = azure.servicemanagement.ServiceManagementService(subscription_id, certificate_path)

    vm_name = host_name 

    logger.info('Creating VM %s of size %s ...' % (vm_name, vm_size))

    # media_link for OS VHD
    timestr = str(datetime.datetime.now().hour) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().minute) + '_' + str(datetime.datetime.now().second)
    media_link = 'https://' + storage_account + '.blob.core.windows.net/' + storage_container + '/' + vm_name + '_' + timestr + '_OS.vhd'

    # Linux configuration set
    linux_config = azure.servicemanagement.LinuxConfigurationSet(host_name, user_name, password, True)

    # SSH public key
    pk = azure.servicemanagement.PublicKey(ssh_certificate_thumbprint, '/home/' + user_name + '/.ssh/authorized_keys')
    pair = azure.servicemanagement.KeyPair(ssh_certificate_thumbprint, '/home/' + user_name + '/.ssh/id_rsa')
    linux_config.ssh.public_keys.public_keys.append(pk)
    linux_config.ssh.key_pairs.key_pairs.append(pair)

    os_hd = azure.servicemanagement.OSVirtualHardDisk(os_image_name, media_link)

    # endpoint configuration set, add SSH port
    ep = azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSet()

    ep.input_endpoints.input_endpoints.append(azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSetInputEndpoint('SSH', 'TCP', str(random.randint(50000, 60000)), '22'))
    ep.input_endpoints.input_endpoints.append(azure.servicemanagement.ConfigurationSetInputEndpoint('Pyro4', 'TCP', str(random.randint(50000, 60000)), '50000', idle_timeout_in_minutes=30))

    # get deployment
    # if the deployment already exists, use AddRole to create new VM, otherwise we should use CreateVirtualMachineDeployment
    new_vm = False
    try:
        deployment = sms.get_deployment_by_name(cloud_service_name, cloud_service_name)
    except azure.WindowsAzureMissingResourceError:
        new_vm = True

    if new_vm == True:
        result = sms.create_virtual_machine_deployment(service_name = cloud_service_name,
                                                    deployment_name = cloud_service_name, 
                                                    deployment_slot = 'production',
                                                    label = cloud_service_name,
                                                    role_size = vm_size,
                                                    role_name = vm_name,
                                                    system_config = linux_config,
                                                    network_config = ep,
                                                    os_virtual_hard_disk = os_hd)
    else:
        result = sms.add_role(service_name = cloud_service_name,
                            deployment_name = cloud_service_name,
                            role_name = vm_name,
                            system_config = linux_config,
                            os_virtual_hard_disk = os_hd,
                            network_config = ep,
                            role_size = vm_size)


    # wait for the async operation to succeed
    request_id = result.request_id

    for i in range(60):
        status = get_operation_status(subscription_id, certificate_path, request_id)

        if status.status == 'Succeeded':
            break
        if status.status == 'Failed':
            print status.error.message
            break

        time.sleep(1)

    result = sms.get_deployment_by_name(cloud_service_name, cloud_service_name)
    logger.info('Deployment ID: %s' % result.private_id)
    return result.private_id

pre-commit takes a strong stance on never modifying the staging area -- you can read more [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/747#issuecomment-386782080) (there's a few other issues as well about running `git add`).

Since software is imperfect in the general case, pre-commit will not commit anything until it has been looked at by a human.

Fortunately, `git add -u && !!` is pretty easy to run if you're ok firing from the hip :)

Yeah, these dependencies are really what Debian packaging calls 'recommends'. We don't have a way to express that distinction in Python packaging, so we make them dependencies.

You can pass a `--no-deps` option to pip to ignore dependencies, and take care of installing them yourself. Of course, you're then responsible for handling any changed dependencies on each upgrade. Or you can install normally and then `pip uninstall` any packages you don't want.

The dump attribute was written to specify that the output is used only for input parsing (parameters), therefore dump_only fields should be removed.

Symmetrically, there could be a load field to specify that the output is only used for output dumping (responses).

But you can't use the same boolean for that, because the output of `schema2jsonschema` can be used for both load and dump: when it is used to create a model definition from a schema. This is what proposed change breaks (see the test I added here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/pull/123).

IOT, just because dump is False, does not mean you can assume the schema is used for loading only. It can be used for both, in which case you want both dump_only and load_only fields to appear.

We could add a symmetrical `load` parameter for those using `schema2jsonschema` to create a response description. We could also rename dump and load into dump_only and load_only if it makes them more explicit (I'm not sure about that, but why not). Now would be the right moment since we already modified dump in a breaking way recently.

Let me share my personal experience. I generally don't parse response schema. In practice, the schemas I use in my application are declared as definitions, as they are used in several places (at least in args load / output dump of the POST method for instance). The only use case I have for a load_only field is a password field. In this case, well, the issue is that writeOnly does not exist as an OpenAPI field attribute. The only way OpenAPI offers to deal with this is to create a second schema without the password field. This is what I do. And doing this, I don't need the change proposed by @luisincrespo.

The gist of it is that you should preallocate dimensions for however many items/users you expect in the future. You can then pass in matrices which have data on users you had no data on before.

The underlying DAC fix was made to SqlClient in PR [#10844](https://github.com/dotnet/corefx/pull/18044). SqlClient is part of the [dotnet/corefx](https://github.com/dotnet/corefx) repo. The fix was then pulled into the [sqltoolsservice](https://github.com/Microsoft/sqltoolsservice) with PR [#466](https://github.com/Microsoft/sqltoolsservice/pull/466). The sqltoolsservice is a reusable micro-service, which mssql-cli uses for all SQL Server communication. In fact, the sqltoolsservice is used by a number of SQL tools:
* [Microsoft/sqlopsstudio](https://github.com/Microsoft/sqlopsstudio)
* [Microsoft/vscode-mssql](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-mssql)
* [Microsoft/mssql-scripter](https://github.com/Microsoft/mssql-scripter)
* [dbcli/mssql-cli](https://github.com/dbcli/mssql-cli)

By making the fix in SqlClient and the sqltoolsservice, all other tools got the fix for free.

@fraoustin I like the idea of coloring the table itself! We have styled output (coloring the table's values), but not the table itself. So, awesome idea!

I am wondering if this would be better as a [preprocessor](https://github.com/dbcli/cli_helpers/blob/master/cli_helpers/tabular_output/preprocessors.py) so it matches the existing code in CLI Helpers. Also, we've generally avoided requiring packages like `click` because that ties CLI Helpers to a particular CLI framework. CLI Helpers is supposed to be framework-agnostic.

That way there is only one output format (e.g. `plain` or `simple`), but you can provide options to color the table grid. You can see the [existing documentation](http://cli-helpers.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#cli_helpers.tabular_output.preprocessors.style_output) for coloring the output.

You can see that the [existing colors/styles](https://github.com/dbcli/cli_helpers/blob/master/cli_helpers/tabular_output/preprocessors.py#L145) in CLI Helpers use [Pygments](http://pygments.org/) to achieve the styling.

It would be really neat if we could use the existing `style_output` preprocessor, but add tokens for coloring the table grid itself. That way, it should be very seamless for clients to adopt it.

What do you think?

The same approach works: `pyvex.IRSB('\x2d\xe9\xf7\x4f', 0x298c9, archinfo.ArchARMEL(), bytes_offset=1)`. VEX will call this instruction `stmdb r13!, {0x4FF7}`, but it's the same as what IDA calls your PUSH.W instruction. The VEX disassembly printer is meant to be cheap.

In general, VEX has extremely good instruction set coverage, better than pretty much any other IR, since Valgrind has to be able to lift any code it analyzes.

So, good news is that I found the error. 
The inner anonymous structures were originally initialized like this:
```.c
.condition.rule             = COND_NEVER,
```
This left all the other descriptors in `condition` undefined. If I use a designated initializer for the inner structure with a trailing comma, all unlisted designators are initialized to their defaults.
```.c
.condition                  = {
     .rule                   = COND_NEVER,
},
```
Since these structures have static storage, it seems that gcc 4.9 is not implementing c99 section 6.7.8:10 correctly, but it is unlikely that it will change anytime soon. I think this initialization is relaxed in gcc 5.

The bad news is that gcc 4's optimizers are not as good as gcc 5's.
Building the `ncp` image overruns the flash boundary by 308 bytes. I suggest disabling the build for cc2650 on gcc 4.9. And I'll submit another pull request for a build configuration only for the `mtd` image.

>> This isn't actually a problem with the recommended Spinel SPI protocol. If the slave is just waking up and isn't ready to transmit, the slave would keep MISO high. 

Effectively the MISO pin becomes another handshake signal.

An important thing to to note is handshake signals may be active low, or active high depending upon the actual HW architecture in the system.  Active high signals (ie: 0V is the idle state) are more power hungry only due to parasitic leakage in the HW design, and very anti-battery life. A few micro amps integrated over a long sleep time adds up. To paraphrase Yogi Berra - A few microamps here, and a few microamps there now you are talking about real money. Another problem you can have is the VDD-IO voltage rails may change level in sleep mode, remember power (watts) is a driven by voltage (e) more specifically e^2, so being able to drop voltage during sleep is important,  and 0^2 is always 0.  Changing VDD-IO presents other problems if the signal is active low, where as an active high signal does not. Momentary over voltage in those situations is cheaply solved with an inline resistor.

Some SOCs provide pin-observation at all times, others do not, which means: Some hosts must dynamically reconfigure the MISO pin, which is not always simple from user space (ie: Linux Device tree nonsense)

>> [in the uart case] When the NCP has something to send to the master while flow control is asserted, it will occasionally send a UART "break"

Interesting concept...  In my experience UART break detection does not work very well across all the micro controller that I have used in projects. Some do well, some do horribly it is very hit or miss.  Thus I have always avoided this.

>> Theoretically RESET isn't required, but we have found that it is useful for the master to have the ability to force a hardware reset of the slave out-of-band.

Agreed - also needed for UART mode to do 'in-place' flash update of the device from the linux host.

>> It seems like you are proposing that we change the SPI protocol to require a two-way handshake

yes exactly. This is also needed for UART mode. I probably was not very clear.

>> It makes the implementation that drives the master from Linux user-space more complex

not really, it works like this:

# Send Steps

Send Step 0:
  DO NOT assert READY yet
  Perform a MUTEX lock in the IO scheme.
  DISARM the RX request logic (ie: edge irq)

  Power up IO circuit, 
  Enable clocks in all required power domains.

SEND Step 1:
  sender  assert MY_READY  (master or slave)
  (set GPIO pin)

SEND Step 2:
  sender wait    OTHER_READY
  (poll GPIO pin)

SEND Step 3:
  Transfer is performed (uart or spi)
  In spi case the Linux host starts clocking the SPI_CLOCK pin.
  In linux user space this is just a call to the SPI transfer function.

SEND Step 4:
  to handle race condition:
      Sender must examine other reply
      in UART case - if no bytes - no message
      in SPI case - if all bytes = STUFFING value no message

  If "other_message exists" and is not yet complete
  The host must complete this message:

  Example SPI case
       HOST only clocked in/out 20 bytes, 
       And the other message is longer...
       The SPI interface will not complete the transfer
       The host must send "stuffing values" to produce transfer clocks.

  Example UART case
       typically nothing to do here
       What you don't want to do is shut down the IO power yet.

   If the message was completed - post the message into the RX thread.

SEND Step 5:
   REARM the RX request logic, this must occur before dropping request.
   Sender DROPS request.

SEND step 6:
    Note: Do not wait until receiver Drops READY
    This might never happen, you might be too slow and miss this.

SEND step 7:
    Release Mutex lock on the IO scheme

SEND step 8:
     Done.

# Rx Step

RX STEP 1:  Wait for REQUEST signal..
       Note this is an *EDGE* request, not a level request.

RX STEP 2:   MUTEX LOCK transfer scheme.

RX STEP 3:  Power up IO circuit, enable clocks, set IO voltages.

RX STEP 4:   Assert READY (this is the request to other side)

RX STEP 5:  Wait for others to assert ready.
       
RX STEP 5:  Transfer is performed.
        If using SPI, the the receiver must send "stuffing values" 
        Either all FF or all 00

RX STEP 6:  End of transfer
       RE-ARM rx-edge detect.
       DROP ready signal.
       Release Mutex

RX STEP 7:
       Post RX message received.

I've updated the PR; copying and pasting the main commit message here:

This extends py::vectorize to automatically pass through non-vectorizable arguments.  This removes the need for the documented "explicitly exclude an argument" workaround.

Vectorization now applies to arithmetic, std::complex, and POD types, passed as plain value or by const lvalue reference (previously only pass-by-value types were supported).  Non-const lvalue references and any other types are passed through as-is.

Functions with rvalue reference arguments (whether vectorizable or not) are explicitly prohibited: an rvalue reference is inherently not something that can be passed multiple times and is thus unsuitable to being in a vectorized function.

The vectorize returned value is also now more sensitive to inputs: previously it would return by value when all inputs are of size 1; this is now amended to having all inputs of size 1 *and* 0 dimensions.  Thus if you pass in, for example, [[1]], you get back a 1x1, 2D array, while previously you got back just the resulting single value.

Vectorization of member function specializations is now also supported via `py::vectorize(&Class::method)`; this required passthrough support for the initial object pointer on the wrapping function pointer.

This commit also removes the documentation about manually dealing with buffers; that feature is much better covered by the direct access implementation (which is the subsequent section).

Based on tests so far, joblib from scikit-learn cannot make scikit-rebate have multiprocessing in eithor Python 2 or Windows environment.

The main issue is that the pickle in python 2 do not supported instance method in class (but the pickle in python 3 naively supported instance method). I also tried some workaround mentioned in this [link](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27318290/why-can-i-pass-an-instance-method-to-multiprocessing-process-but-not-a-multipro). It can make instance method pickleable but joblib only use 1 core instead of multiple cores.

One solution to put multiprocessing in python 2 for scikit-rebate is to put [_get_row_missing](https://github.com/EpistasisLab/scikit-rebate/blob/master/skrebate/relieff.py#L294-L325) and [_compute_scores](https://github.com/EpistasisLab/scikit-rebate/blob/master/skrebate/relieff.py#L361-L366) outside of class. I make [a branch](https://github.com/weixuanfu2016/scikit-rebate/tree/test_sklearn_joblib) in my forked repo to test the solution in `ReliefF`. It works well in python 2.7. Need more workaround for Windows and other algorithms in rebate.

Since then, I think I now would prefer to add methods to the main class that return the results you want. It's more flexible and doesn't attach hooks everywhere in your model.
For example, a feature extractor using ResNets
```python
from torchvision import models

class ResNetFeatureExtractor(models.ResNet):
    def __init__(self, block, layers, num_classes):
        super(ResNetFeatureExtractor, self).__init__(
              block=block, layers=layers, num_classes=1000)  # for ImageNet

        self.my_classifier = nn.Conv2d(128, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.conv1(x)
        x = self.bn1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        x = self.maxpool(x)

        l1 = self.layer1(x)
        l2 = self.layer2(l1)
        x = self.my_classifier(l2)

        return x, l1, l2

def my_pretrained_resnet18(num_classes):
    block = models.resnet.BasicBlock
    model = ResNetFeatureExtractor(block, [2, 2, 2, 2], num_classes)
    model_url = models.resnet.model_urls['resnet18']
    model.load_state_dict(model_zoo.load_url(model_url), strict=False)
    return model
```

And you can easily load any models using `load_state_dict`, and can even add new layers by using `strict=False` in `load_state_dict`. You can also add extra input arguments to the constructor that make it more flexible to you to return specific layers, etc.
I think this solution is slightly more verbose, but more flexible as well and I think its my favorite way at the moment.

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest and report.

Yes, this is an issue with the `process_startup` function. For shell requests, no message should be sent but the function does not allow that. An empty string as the message (as opposed to no message), is not accepted by the server which terminates the connection.

The next version will have a `channel.shell()` function which was missing - the libssh2 macro for shell process startup - as well as a revised `process_startup` which allows for empty messages.

From next version onwards:

```
channel = session.open_session()
channel.shell()
channel.write('my command\n')
size, data = channel.read()
<..>
```

Note that a pty is not explicitly required for interactive shells though it may be used.

Unfortunately using base_viewset is not completly solving my issue.
My viewset has the method get_serializer_class overriden to use different serializers for read/write operations. I get the serializer I want using the action attribute of the view (create/update/list or metadata in our case, default to list serializer).
When using the base_viewset, the wrong serializer will be used when determining metadata as it was instantiated along with the endpoint (so without the action).
I have the same issue actually even when using viewset which I solved by doing this in determine_metadata:
```
        if hasattr(view, 'endpoint'):
            endpoint = view.endpoint
            endpoint.serializer = view.get_serializer_class()
```
The same trick is not fully working with base_viewset, as the base list of fields is wrong. (There's no serializer defined when getting the list so all the fields are fetched), so in get_field_dict even if the serializer is correct there I end up will all the fields anyway because of this part:
```
    try:
        field_instance = serializer_instance.fields[name]
    except KeyError:
        return {'key': name}
```
Though I can still filter them in the adapter then because the type is not set then.

Summing up:
- With a dynamic get_queryset_class on a viewset, the wrong serializer is used when determining_metadata (using viewset or base_viewset)
- When using base_viewset the list of fields is also wrongly generated (but can be handled in the adapter)
- When using a viewset a fresh new endpoint is generated to determine_metadata, losing the customisation of the original one (original issue)

@dzhulgakov, great analysis of the proposal. Thanks for writing up your thoughts.

FWIW, we considered your proposal D above before putting this up here (and I would definitely do D over C). 

The upside of D over the current payload is:
1. The spec is *way* simpler - no "protobuf in protobuf." 
1. The PB files will be smaller - especially for dense graphs. 

The downside of D over the current payload is: 
1. Attribute values become completely opaque to frameworks/runtimes. Not fatal, but this definitely limits the ability to do any pre-processing/validation of attribute values prior to initializing the operator, which is the only piece of code that can parse the blob.
2. When we eventually do an operator schema, we can address the previous issue, but that will potentially push the complexity around, not reduce overall complexity. 
3. If we look at the DNN operators in CoreML, a bunch of the attributes will become blobs.  For better or worse, one framework/runtime made a different stylistic choice from Caffe2. 
4. Once we allow non-tensor values as input/output values, we are going to wind up needing at least the TypeProto additions. If we allow non-tensor values as constants and/or initializer values, we'll wind up with ValueProto.

I'm sensitive to the desire to stratify that spec based on DNN vs. non-DNN operators. That's not going to save us from solving the structured attribute problem and, at least for image, some form of additional typing on input/output values.

"There are two ways to leave an optional input or output unspecified: the first, available only for trailing inputs and outputs, is to simply not provide that input; the second method is to use an empty string in place of an input or output name." - from https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/IR.md

# Usage examples for image classification models

## Classify ImageNet classes with VGG16
```python
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 224, 224, 3])

# get the whole model
vgg = tl.models.VGG16(x)

# restore pre-trained VGG parameters
vgg.restore_params()

# use pre-trained VGG
softmax = tf.nn.softmax(vgg.outputs)
...
```

## Extract features with VGG16 and Train a classifier with 100 classes

```python
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 224, 224, 3])

# get VGG without the last layer
vgg = tl.models.VGG16(x, end_with=‘fc2_relu’)

# add one more layer
network = tl.layers.DenseLayer(vgg, 100, name=‘out’)

# initialize all parameters
tl.layers.initialize_global_variables(sess)

# restore pre-trained VGG parameters
vgg.restore_params()

# train your own classifier (only update the last layer)
train_parames = tl.layers.get_variable_with_name(‘out’) 
…
```

## Reuse model

```python
x1 = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 224, 224, 3])

x2 = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 224, 224, 3])

vgg1 = tl.models.VGG16(x, end_with=‘fc2_relu’)

# reuse parameters of vgg1, but use different input
vgg2 = tl.models.VGG16(x, end_with=‘fc2_relu’, reuse=True)

# restore pre-trained VGG parameters (as they share parameters, don’t need to restore vgg2)
vgg1.restore_params()
```

I think the usual procedure is the convert the RGB image to grayscale, hence removing a dimension.
A practical implementation of an agent in an RGB environment is here. (I haven't trained it myself, but it's an example for everyone).
https://github.com/keras-rl/keras-rl/blob/master/examples/dqn_atari.py
This also involves using the history frames in place of the RGB channels. In your case you appear to pass 1 history frame (no history, only current, inferring from `(1, 1, 3, 144, 256)`, the second `1`). In this case your CNN may have `channels_first` with `1` channel, shape `(?, 1, 144, 256)`, OR, `channels_first` with `3` channels (RGB) since you're not utilising history `(?, 3, 144, 256)`.

*Note: I'm inferring `(Batch Size, History, RGB, Pixels, Pixels)`*

Here is a little snippet from the attached sample (line 24 to 31)
```py
class AtariProcessor(Processor):
    def process_observation(self, observation):
        assert observation.ndim == 3  # (height, width, channel)
        img = Image.fromarray(observation)
        img = img.resize(INPUT_SHAPE).convert('L')  # resize and convert to grayscale
        processed_observation = np.array(img)
        assert processed_observation.shape == INPUT_SHAPE
        return processed_observation.astype('uint8')  # saves storage in experience memory
```

```python
>>> parser = Lark(r'''
... input                : word+
... word                 : unquoted | lquoted | squoted | dquoted
...
... lquoted               : LQUOTED
... squoted               : /'[^']*'/
... dquoted               : /"(?:[^"]+|\\.)*"/
... unquoted              : UNQUOTED
...
... LQUOTED.2: /\$'[^']*'/
... UNQUOTED: /(?:[^\s()&;|<>]+|\\.)+/
...
... %import common.WS
... %ignore WS
... ''', start='input', parser='lalr')

>>> print(parser.parse("$'abcd'").pretty())
input
  word
    lquoted	$'abcd'


>>> print(parser.parse("abcd").pretty())
input
  word
    unquoted	abcd
```
